---
title: DOXMOX - monitoring Proxmox VE guide changes with GitHub Actions

tags: [proxmox, monitoring, github-actions, python, automation]

---

_Hello, World!_


I noticed a simple pattern: **before an update**, or before a release that impacts how Proxmox VE works, **the documentation gets updated too**. Which makes sense.

I wanted to track changes in the Proxmox VE guide. The need is simple: get notified when the content really changes, keep a readable trace of the differences, and be able to review the history later.

This bot exists to see those documentation changes **as soon as they appear**.

I built `DOXMOX` for this case. (Project link: [doxmox.com](https://doxmox.com/) ) The project runs on GitHub Actions **every X hours**, compares the current version with the previous one, then publishes a static changelog.

> `DOXMOX` does not predict Proxmox VE releases. It detects documentation changes and provides an actionable signal to check what is changing before updates.
{: .prompt-info }

---

## The actual pipeline

The script downloads the source page with `requests` and handles the classic network errors (`429`, `5xx`) with retries.
The HTML is then normalized: selecting the useful areas (`header`, `toc`, `content`, `footer`) and removing noise (`script`, `noscript`).

On this normalized content, `doxmox` computes a **SHA-256**. If the hash has not changed, **the run stops**. If the hash changes, the script generates a text diff (`docs/changes/<timestamp>.diff`), its HTML version, a new entry in `data/history.json`, then updates `docs/index.html`.

The workflow commits and pushes the artifacts, then sends the Telegram alert. The initial bootstrap sends **no alert** to avoid noise.

![Example doxmox Telegram alert](/img/doxmox/tg_doxmox.png){: .shadow }

---

## Why this design holds up in production

Comparing raw HTML creates **a lot of false positives**. A banner change, attribute reordering, or a JS block breaks the hash with no impact on the docs.
Normalization removes that noise. The hash therefore reflects **the useful text**.

The diff archive saves me time. When an alert arrives, I don't need to re-read the whole source page. I open `docs/index.html`, click the entry, and **immediately see the difference** between two versions.

For maintenance, I kept two paths to clean up the history:
via the admin workflow for an action from GitHub, or via CLI when I want to act locally.

---

## What I use day to day

The project is in production on GitHub Actions + GitHub Pages. In practice, it serves me for **three concrete actions**.

- I quickly check whether the change affects critical procedures (*installation, network, storage*).
- I keep a **dated history** of what changed, useful when I have to explain why an internal procedure was modified.
- I cut down manual monitoring time: I read the diffs **only when there is a real change**.

> When the docs don't change, `DOXMOX` doesn't notify. The goal is to reduce noise, not to generate a permanent stream of alerts.
{: .prompt-tip }

---

## Known limitations

The project depends on the HTML structure of the source page. If that structure changes significantly, the normalization has to be adapted.
The diff is textual. It **does not understand the semantics** of the content.
The scope is deliberately simple: **one source, one history, one static site**.

Project link: [doxmox.com](https://doxmox.com/)

---

## Update — May 2026

Since April, the AI pipeline has changed in three ways.

The changelog is still generated in English first, then translated to `ru` and `fr`. Translation is now decoupled from the full backfill. This avoids re-running the whole analysis when only `ru/fr` is missing.

Workflows used:

- `history-ai-backfill.yml` to regenerate a full entry;
- `history-ai-translate.yml` to translate only (`en -> ru/fr`);
- `history-ai-brief.yml` to generate a short line from the already-present EN changelog.

The `brief` mode produces a line like this:

`Proxmox VE 9.2.1: Ceph, CPU model, Regex, Container ID, HA auto-rebalance, Node location, Backup, ZFS`

This line is added to `docs/index.html` (the *Details* column) and to the Telegram message.

Models used:

- analysis: `@cf/openai/gpt-oss-120b`
- analysis fallback: `@cf/moonshotai/kimi-k2.6`
- translation: `@cf/zai-org/glm-4.7-flash`
- short summary: `@cf/openai/gpt-oss-20b` (fallback `@cf/zai-org/glm-4.7-flash`)
- embeddings: `@cf/baai/bge-m3`

In practice, to limit AI costs:

- use `translate` if the EN changelog already exists;
- use `brief` to recompute only the short line;
- keep `backfill --force` for cases where everything really needs regenerating.

Detailed technical documentation is in `docs/AI_PIPELINE.md`.

Thanks!
